title: Improving FMQA via Initial Training Data Design Considering Marginal Bit Coverage in One-Hot Encoding
authors: Taiga Hayashi, Yuya Seki, Kotaro Terada, Yosuke Mukasa, Shuta Kikuchi, Shu Tanaka
abstract: Factorization machine with quadratic-optimization annealing (FMQA) is a black-box optimization method that combines a factorization machine (FM) surrogate with QUBO-based search by an Ising machine. When FMQA is applied to integer or discretized continuous variables via one-hot encoding, uniform random initial sampling can leave many binary variables never active in the initial training data, and the corresponding FM parameters receive no direct gradient updates from the observed responses. We address this by designing the initial training data to achieve complete marginal bit coverage, namely, ensuring that every binary variable obtained by one-hot encoding takes the value one at least once. We use two space-filling sampling methods, Latin hypercube sampling (LHS) and the Sobol' sequence, yielding LHS-FMQA and Sobol'-FMQA. On the human-powered aircraft wing-shape optimization benchmark with 17 and 32 design variables, both proposed methods achieved numerically higher mean final cruising speeds than the baseline FMQA, with the advantage more pronounced on the 32-variable problem.
summary: This paper improves the FMQA optimization framework by ensuring that all binary variables in a one-hot encoded system are represented in the initial training dataset. By using Latin Hypercube and Sobol' sampling, the authors prevent biased parameter updates, leading to more effective searches on Ising machines.
main_problem: In Factorization Machine with Quadratic-optimization Annealing (FMQA), one-hot encoding of variables can lead to 'uncovered' binary bits that never take the value one in the initial training data, causing vanishing gradients and biased QUBO coefficients.
main_result: The proposed LHS-FMQA and Sobol'-FMQA methods, which ensure complete marginal bit coverage, achieve higher mean final cruising speeds in wing-shape optimization benchmarks compared to the baseline FMQA.
method: The authors use space-filling sampling techniques, specifically Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) and Sobol' sequences, to design initial training data that guarantees every binary variable in the one-hot encoding is activated at least once.
model_or_system: The framework uses a Factorization Machine (FM) as a surrogate model for black-box optimization, which is then mapped to a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) form compatible with Ising machine hardware.
key_observables: Final mean cruising speed (m/s) and the optimization gain (improvement over initial best value).
important_parameters: Number of design variables (17 and 32), number of discrete levels (M=32), and initial training sample size (N0).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the use of one-hot encoding for discretized variables and focuses on marginal bit coverage rather than pairwise bit coverage.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the optimization progress for a 17-variable problem; Figures 3 and 4 show the distribution of bit occurrences, highlighting the reduction of 'never active' bits in the proposed methods.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the gradient vanishing problem in FMQA, proposes LHS and Sobol' sampling methods to ensure marginal bit coverage, validates these methods using HPA wing-shape optimization benchmarks, and discusses limitations regarding pairwise coverage and space-filling effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Comparison of the optimization processes of each method for HPA103-1. The horizontal axis represents the number of function evalua- tions, and the vertical axis represents the best value found. The gray shaded region corresponds to the evaluations used to construct the initial training dataset.

Fig. 2. Comparison of the optimization processes of each method for HPA103-2. The horizontal axis represents the number of function evalua- tions, and the vertical axis represents the best value found. The gray shaded region corresponds to the evaluations used to construct the initial training dataset.

Fig. 4. Comparison of the number of times each bit takes the value one in the bit arrays obtained by one-hot encoding for the datasets generated by (a) Conv-FMQA, (b) LHS-FMQA, and (c) Sobol’-FMQA on HPA103-2. The horizontal axis represents the number of function evaluations, and the vertical axis represents the distribution of the number of times each bit in the dataset takes the value one at each function evaluation. Red indicates bits that never take the value one, green indicates bits that take the value one only once, blue indicates bits that take the value one two to nine times, and black indicates bits that take the value one ten or more times.

Fig. 3. Comparison of the number of times each bit takes the value one in the bit arrays obtained by one-hot encoding for the datasets generated by (a) Conv-FMQA, (b) LHS-FMQA, and (c) Sobol’-FMQA on HPA103-1. The horizontal axis represents the number of function evaluations, and the vertical axis represents the distribution of the number of times each bit in the dataset takes the value one at each function evaluation. Red indicates bits that never take the value one, green indicates bits that take the value one only once, blue indicates bits that take the value one two to nine times, and black indicates bits that take the value one ten or more times.
